archetypes.name: Sensory Intake
archetypes.description: Direct perceptual schema loading through concrete observation and iconic representation.

designHints.opening: Present a concrete phenomenon the learner can see or touch. Use clear visual aids (barGraph, numberLine) to anchor the schema.
designHints.assessment: Use quiz or fill_blank to verify recognition. Keep assessment at recall level — the learner should identify, not analyze.
designHints.visual: Static SVG factories (barGraph, venn, numberLine) work well. Sensor binding is optional at this level.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid abstract definitions or lengthy text. P1 at B1-B2 means the learner absorbs through direct sensory exposure, not reading.

archetypes.name: Embodied Discovery
archetypes.description: Learner discovers a concept through physical manipulation and sensor measurement of a surprising phenomenon.

designHints.opening: Begin with a physical action that produces a counter-intuitive observation. The learner's body is the instrument — tilt, shake, drop, rotate.
designHints.assessment: Prefer ordering or matching over MCQ. The learner should demonstrate understanding by reconstructing the observed pattern, not selecting from options.
designHints.visual: Use sensor-driven SVG (timeGraph, polygonDynamic, numberLineDynamic) so structure emerges from motion. The visual IS the epistemic instrument.
designHints.antipatterns: Never explain the phenomenon before the learner encounters it. Avoid quiz steps before the exploration phase — let disequilibrium do its work.

archetypes.name: Motor Apprenticeship
archetypes.description: Procedural skill building through repeated sensor-verified physical practice with progressive thresholds.

designHints.opening: Demonstrate the target physical action once (instruction step), then immediately have the learner attempt it with sensor verification.
designHints.assessment: Use progressive hardware_trigger steps with increasing threshold precision. The final trigger should require fluid execution, not just threshold crossing.
designHints.visual: Use numberLineDynamic or timeGraph to give real-time feedback on approach to target. The learner should see their accuracy improve across attempts.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid mimicry without understanding — pair with a brief ordering step that verifies the learner grasps WHY the procedure works, not just HOW to execute it.

archetypes.name: Embodied Sequencing
archetypes.description: Stabilization of embodied skills through sequenced practice that varies context while preserving the core operation.

designHints.opening: Present a familiar physical task in a new configuration. The learner should recognize the underlying skill but need to adapt their approach.
designHints.assessment: Alternate between hardware_trigger (execution) and ordering/matching (reflection). The learner must both perform and reason about sequence.
designHints.visual: Use compose layers to show multiple representations simultaneously — e.g., sensor graph + structural diagram.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid rigid repetition of identical tasks. P4 at B1-B2 means varied practice, not drill. If every hardware_trigger has the same threshold, you're doing P3.

archetypes.name: Concrete Transfer
archetypes.description: Applying embodied knowledge to a novel real-world scenario that requires recognizing familiar structure in unfamiliar context.

designHints.opening: Frame a scenario that seems different from previous lessons but contains the same structural core. The learner must discover the connection through action.
designHints.assessment: Use matching to connect old context with new context. The learner should pair observations from the transfer scenario with familiar structures.
designHints.visual: Use arrowMap or compose to overlay familiar visual structures onto the new scenario. Show that the underlying pattern is invariant.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid explicitly telling the learner 'this is like what you did before.' Transfer must be discovered, not announced. Risk: Activity without Rigor.

archetypes.name: Procedural Intake
archetypes.description: Schema loading for operational procedures through worked examples and structured demonstration.

designHints.opening: Present a worked example that demonstrates the complete procedure. Use a visual scaffold (barGraph, cartesianGrid) to make the procedural structure visible.
designHints.assessment: Use fill_blank to verify the learner can reproduce key steps. Follow with a quiz to confirm terminology/concepts. Keep at recognition level.
designHints.visual: Use static SVGs (barGraph, cartesianGrid, numberLine) that illustrate the procedure's structure. Annotate steps visually.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid pure text walls. Even at P1/B3-B4, the procedure should be shown, not just described. Risk: Passive Familiarity Illusion.

archetypes.name: Structural Construction
archetypes.description: Building relational understanding through guided exploration of patterns, connections, and structural properties.

designHints.opening: Present two or more related structures and let the learner discover what they share. Use visual exploration (cartesianGrid, compose) to make relationships navigable.
designHints.assessment: Use ordering to test whether the learner grasps the underlying sequence/hierarchy. Use matching to test whether they see structural equivalences.
designHints.visual: Use cartesianGrid, compose layers, or venn diagrams. The visual must expose the relational structure, not just display data.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid giving the structure away in the instruction. The learner must construct the relationship — P2 means the schema is built, not received.

archetypes.name: Procedural Fluency
archetypes.description: Building execution speed and accuracy through deliberate practice with progressive difficulty and immediate feedback.

designHints.opening: Brief recap of the procedure (1 instruction step), then immediately begin practice. The learner should spend most of the lesson executing, not reading.
designHints.assessment: Interleave fill_blank (procedural execution) and quiz (conceptual check). Increase difficulty across the sequence. Use step weights to emphasize later items.
designHints.visual: Minimal visual scaffolding — the focus is on execution. Use numberLine or barGraph only to show progress or accuracy trends.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid pure drill without conceptual checkpoints. P3 risks Mimicry — intersperse at least one quiz that verifies the learner understands the 'why' behind the procedure.

archetypes.name: Relational Sequencing
archetypes.description: Stabilizing structural understanding through varied practice that requires recognizing the same relation across different representations.

designHints.opening: Present the same structural relationship in a new representation. Ask a question (Socratic) that forces the learner to reconcile the two views.
designHints.assessment: Use matching across representations (e.g., pair a graph with an equation, pair a table with a diagram). Use ordering to test sequencing of structural transformations.
designHints.visual: Use compose to juxtapose two representations. cartesianGrid paired with barGraph, or venn with numberLine.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid testing only one representation. P4 at B3-B4 means the learner must see the same structure from multiple angles. Risk: Lockstep Stagnation if all tasks use the same format.

archetypes.name: Applied Problem Solving
archetypes.description: Transferring operational/structural knowledge to authentic, multi-step problems that require selecting and combining procedures.

designHints.opening: Present an authentic scenario (real-world framing or narrative) where the learner must choose which tools/procedures apply. Do not cue which procedure to use.
designHints.assessment: Use a mix of step types. The learner must: identify the right approach (quiz), connect problem features to solution structures (matching), execute the procedure (fill_blank), and justify the sequence (ordering).
designHints.visual: Use scenario-appropriate visuals — arrowMap for processes, cartesianGrid for quantitative reasoning, compose for multi-faceted problems.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid over-scaffolding. P5 at B3-B4 means the learner must deploy skills in context. If the step sequence telegraphs which procedure to use, it degenerates to P3. Risk: Activity without Rigor.

archetypes.name: Formal Schema
archetypes.description: Introducing abstract frameworks, formal definitions, and systemic models through structured exposition.

designHints.opening: Present the formal framework with precise language and visual scaffolding. Use equations (KaTeX) and structural diagrams to make the abstraction concrete.
designHints.assessment: Use fill_blank for definitional recall and quiz for conceptual verification. The learner should be able to reproduce key formalisms.
designHints.visual: Use cartesianGrid, unitCircle, or compose to visualize the formal structure. Equations rendered via KaTeX should be integrated with visual representations.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid treating B5-B6 as 'harder B3-B4.' Formal schema requires explicit modeling of the abstract structure — don't just present harder numbers. Risk: Passive Familiarity Illusion at the symbolic level.

archetypes.name: Hypothesis Testing
archetypes.description: Constructing and testing formal hypotheses through prediction, experimentation, and evidence evaluation.

designHints.opening: Present a formal claim or model and ask the learner to predict what would happen under specific conditions. The prediction (quiz) must come BEFORE any data collection.
designHints.assessment: Use ordering to sequence evidence, fill_blank to formalize conclusions. The learner should confront prediction failures and revise their model.
designHints.visual: Use timeGraph or cartesianGrid to display experimental data. Use compose to overlay the predicted model against actual observations.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid confirmation bias — design the experiment so naive predictions are WRONG. P2 at B5-B6 requires genuine cognitive restructuring, not validation of existing understanding.

archetypes.name: Formal Practice
archetypes.description: Developing fluency with formal reasoning procedures through structured symbolic manipulation and proof-like activities.

designHints.opening: Present the formal procedure with one worked example, then immediately begin practice. Focus on symbolic manipulation — the learner should internalize the formal operations.
designHints.assessment: Use fill_blank for symbolic completion (fill in the missing step in a derivation). Use ordering for proof sequencing. Quiz only for metacognitive checks.
designHints.visual: Use KaTeX-rendered equations. cartesianGrid or unitCircle for visual verification of symbolic results.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid pure symbol pushing without meaning. Include at least one step that connects the formal procedure to a concrete interpretation. P3 at B5-B6 risks Mimicry at the symbolic level.

archetypes.name: Systematic Consolidation
archetypes.description: Stabilizing formal understanding by requiring the learner to navigate multiple formal representations and reconcile them systematically.

designHints.opening: Present the same formal concept via two different formalisms or notations. Ask the learner to reconcile them (Socratic: 'Are these the same thing? Prove it.').
designHints.assessment: Use matching to pair equivalent representations. Use ordering to sequence a logical argument. fill_blank for completing the bridging step between formalisms.
designHints.visual: Use compose to display parallel representations. cartesianGrid vs. algebraic form, or geometric vs. analytic views of the same object.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid testing only one formalism. P4 at B5-B6 is about systemic stability — the learner must hold multiple formal views simultaneously. Risk: Lockstep Stagnation if tasks are all from one perspective.

archetypes.name: Authentic Transfer
archetypes.description: Deploying formal reasoning in high-stakes, realistic scenarios that demand judgment, not just execution.

designHints.opening: Present an authentic scenario (historical event, engineering problem, ethical dilemma, scientific controversy) where formal reasoning is required but insufficient alone. The learner must combine formal tools with contextual judgment.
designHints.assessment: Use matching to connect formal models to scenario features. Use ordering to construct an argument or solution path. fill_blank for formalizing the conclusion. Quiz for metacognitive reflection.
designHints.visual: Use scenario-appropriate visuals — arrowMap for causal reasoning, compose for multi-perspective analysis. Visuals should represent the scenario, not just the math.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid scenarios where the 'right answer' is obvious. P5 at B5-B6 requires genuine epistemic challenge — multiple valid approaches, trade-offs, and uncertainty. Risk: Activity without Rigor.

archetypes.name: Sensor Lab
archetypes.description: Sensor-centric scientific investigation where the phone becomes a measurement instrument and the learner collects, visualizes, and interprets real data.

designHints.opening: Set up a physical investigation: the learner positions their phone as a scientific instrument. The first hardware_trigger verifies they've produced a measurable phenomenon.
designHints.assessment: Use a second hardware_trigger that requires reproducing a specific physical state (demonstrating understanding). Follow with ordering or matching to verify interpretation of the collected data.
designHints.visual: Use timeGraph for streaming sensor data. Use compose to overlay multiple sensor streams. The visual record IS the data — the learner reads the SVG like a scientist reads a chart.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid treating the sensor as a novelty. The measurement must reveal something invisible to casual observation. If the sensor step could be replaced by 'look at the object,' the design is too shallow.

archetypes.name: Socratic Dialogue
archetypes.description: Question-driven exploration where each step poses a challenge that forces the learner to confront gaps in their reasoning.

designHints.opening: Open with a provocative question or apparent paradox. The first quiz captures the learner's initial intuition (which should often be wrong). The follow-up instruction reveals why.
designHints.assessment: Interleave questions (quiz) with reflection tasks (fill_blank, matching). Each question should build on the previous answer, creating a chain of reasoning.
designHints.visual: Use visuals that expose contradictions — e.g., two cartesianGrid plots that appear different but represent the same data, or a venn diagram that challenges set membership intuitions.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid leading questions with obvious 'right' answers. Socratic dialogue requires genuine intellectual tension. If every question has one clearly correct option, it's didactic with extra steps.

archetypes.name: Narrative Journey
archetypes.description: Story-framed exploration where a narrative context motivates observation, measurement, and reasoning about a phenomenon.

designHints.opening: Establish a brief narrative frame (a character, a setting, a problem). The story should make the learning objective feel like a natural part of the plot, not an imposed task.
designHints.assessment: Use matching to connect story elements to structural concepts. The learner should see that the narrative and the model describe the same reality.
designHints.visual: Use arrowMap or compose to visualize the story's causal structure alongside the formal model. Let the narrative drive the visual exploration.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid using narrative as decoration. The story must be load-bearing — removing it should make the lesson harder, not easier. Risk: Activity without Rigor if the story replaces the cognitive challenge.

archetypes.name: Cross-Domain Bridge
archetypes.description: Connecting skills across UTU spines (MAC↔SCI, SCI↔SOC, MAC↔SOC) to build interdisciplinary transfer.

designHints.opening: Present a problem from domain B that requires a skill from domain A. The learner must recognize that a familiar tool applies in an unfamiliar context.
designHints.assessment: Use matching to explicitly connect concepts across domains. Use ordering to build a chain of reasoning that crosses the spine boundary.
designHints.visual: Use compose to display domain A's representation alongside domain B's problem. Visually highlight the structural isomorphism.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid superficial 'math in science' connections. The bridge must be structural — the same reasoning pattern must genuinely operate in both domains. Theta modifier is high (1.2) because cross-domain coherence is especially valuable.

archetypes.name: Scaffolded Retry
archetypes.description: Failure-recovery pattern with progressive hints that maintain cognitive demand while reducing frustration.

designHints.opening: Present the core task directly. Use the first assessment step as a diagnostic — its on_fail routing sends the learner to a scaffold, not a dead end.
designHints.assessment: Design two parallel assessment paths: the main path (higher difficulty) and the scaffold path (same concept, lower cognitive load). Both paths converge at the completion step.
designHints.visual: Use the same visual in both paths but with additional annotations in the scaffold path. The learner should see what they missed, not receive a different task.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid making the scaffold path trivially easy. The retry should maintain the learning objective while reducing extraneous cognitive load. The theta modifier is slightly below 1.0 because scaffold-heavy lessons indicate the learner may need prerequisite review.

archetypes.name: Peer Collaborative
archetypes.description: Group-designed lesson where multiple learners contribute to a shared investigation, requiring coordination and peer verification.

designHints.opening: Frame a task that requires multiple data points from different physical positions or perspectives. Each group member contributes a measurement or observation.
designHints.assessment: Use matching or ordering to synthesize group data. The final instruction step should include a manual_verification gate where a peer or teacher confirms the group's conclusion.
designHints.visual: Use barGraph or compose to aggregate group contributions visually. Each member's data should be distinguishable in the visualization.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid designs where one learner can complete the entire lesson alone. The group dependency must be structural — the task is impossible without multiple contributors. Set meta.is_group: true.

archetypes.name: Rapid Diagnostic
archetypes.description: Quick assessment-only lesson for placement testing, prerequisite verification, or spaced-repetition review.

designHints.opening: Skip the instruction step — begin directly with assessment. The learner already has the relevant schema (this is review/verification, not teaching).
designHints.assessment: Use 3-4 rapid assessment steps that escalate in Bloom's level (remember → understand → apply). Weight later steps higher. Use the results for theta recalibration, not mastery marking.
designHints.visual: Minimal visuals. If used, they should be part of the question, not decoration (e.g., 'which barGraph shows...').
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid teaching in a diagnostic lesson. If the learner needs instruction, theta should route them to a full lesson. Keep total duration under 3 minutes. Theta modifier is low (0.8) because diagnostics don't teach — they measure.

archetypes.name: Design Challenge
archetypes.description: Open-ended creation task where the learner must design, build, or compose a solution that meets specified constraints.

designHints.opening: Present a design brief with clear constraints but no prescribed solution. The learner must plan (fill_blank for design specification), sequence (ordering for implementation steps), and evaluate (matching for constraint verification).
designHints.assessment: Use ordering to test whether the learner can sequence a valid construction plan. Use matching to verify their design meets the stated constraints. The final instruction step presents the design for manual_verification.
designHints.visual: Use compose to provide a workspace visualization. cartesianGrid for quantitative designs, arrowMap for process designs.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid constraining the design so tightly that only one solution is possible. Bloom's 'create' means genuine synthesis. Theta modifier is high (1.2) because creation-level coherence is the strongest signal of deep understanding.

archetypes.name: Multi-Modal Synthesis
archetypes.description: Lesson that deliberately activates all VARK modalities, requiring the learner to translate between sensory channels to build integrated understanding.

designHints.opening: Begin with a kinesthetic experience (sensor interaction), then present the same information visually, then in text. The learner experiences the same concept through three channels before being assessed.
designHints.assessment: Use matching to connect observations across modalities (pair the physical sensation with the visual pattern with the verbal description). Use ordering to sequence the translation chain.
designHints.visual: Use compose layers that present visual and textual information simultaneously. timeGraph for the kinesthetic/sensor channel, barGraph or cartesianGrid for the analytical channel.
designHints.antipatterns: Avoid treating this as three separate mini-lessons. The power of multi-modal synthesis is in the CONNECTIONS between modalities, not in experiencing each independently.
